title: This House to Let
author: Le Queux, William, 1864-1927

quoteFirst: “Houses, or boxes, for receiving letters before four o’clock, at the West end of the town, and five o’clock in the City, are open in every part of the Metropolis; after that hour bell-men collect the letters during another hour, receiving a fee of _one penny_ for each letter; but, at the General Post Office, in Lombard Street, letters are received till seven o’clock; after that, till half an hour after seven, a fee of _sixpence_ must be paid; and from half after seven till
quoteLast: , the postage must be paid, as well as the fee of _sixpence_. Persons, till lately, were, if well known, permitted to have back any letter put in, if required; but, by an order of June, 1802, the masters of receiving houses are not allowed to return letters on any pretence whatever.
title: The Dawn of the XIXth Century in England: A social sketch of the times
author: Ashton, John, 1834-1911

quoteFirst: “It is, I suppose, the satisfaction of depriving yourself of the opportunities of thinking such thoughts that brings you here every morning of the year at
quoteLast: to tidy up the garret of a starving materialist who is bleeding to death of his ideals?”
title: Henry Northcote

quoteLast: we heard the boatswain calling to the sailors in the bows.
title: The Survivors of the Chancellor

quoteFirst: "This note," I remarked, tapping it with my forefinger, "was taken in to Isobel by Mrs. Burdett at
quoteLast: . It was brought, she said, by a respectable middle-aged woman, with whom Isobel left the place soon after eight. We heard of this an hour later. At eleven o'clock we began the search for Monsieur Feurgéres. At three, Allan discovered that he had left the _Savoy Hotel_ at ten for St. Petersburg. Since then we have sent seven telegrams, the delivery of which is very problematical--and we have heard--nothing!"
title: The Master Mummer
author: Oppenheim, E. Phillips (Edward Phillips), 1866-1946
